subject: A Review of Set Theory
question: Consider the experiment of tossing a fair coin n times. Represent the sample space as a Cartesian product.
explanation: If S is the sample space then $S = S_1 \times S_2 \times \ldots \times S_n$, where $S_i$, $1 \le i \le n$, is the set consisting of the two outcomes H = head and T = tail The following theorem is a tool for finding the cardinality of the Cartesian product of two finite sets. Theorem 2.4 Given two finite sets A and B. Then $n(A \times B) = n(A) \cdot n(B)$. Proof. Suppose that $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ and $B = \{b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_m\}$. Then $A \times B = \{(a_1, b_1), (a_1, b_2), \ldots, (a_1, b_m), (a_2, b_1), (a_2, b_2), \ldots, (a_2, b_m), (a_3, b_1), (a_3, b_2), \ldots, (a_3, b_m), \ldots (a_n, b_1), (a_n, b_2), \ldots, (a_n, b_m)\}$ Thus, $n(A \times B) = n \cdot m = n(A) \cdot n(B)$ Remark 2.4 By induction, the previous result can be extended to any finite number of sets.
